planting.note: Plant in spring; a dwarf selection well suited to containers and border fronts.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in rich, moist soil and keep it from drying out. Its short, sturdy habit needs no staking. Deep pink semi-double flowers appear on compact stems from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact selection bred for tidy, mounded growth.

directions: Set the crown flush with the soil and keep evenly moist through summer. The dwarf habit holds its shape without support and suits pots. Bright pink double flowers cover the mound from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in part shade.

directions: Plant with the crown at ground level and mulch to keep roots cool. Leave clumps to establish for the fullest flowering. Rich rose-pink double to semi-double flowers open from late summer through autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in humus-rich soil in dappled shade.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and water deeply until roots take hold. Allow the clump to spread gently into an established stand. Warm rose single flowers with a bowl-shaped form bloom from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this early-blooming form starts flowering ahead of most.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in retentive soil and keep watered in dry spells. Enjoy its head start on the anemone season. Rosy-pink single flowers appear from mid to late summer and carry on into fall.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in humus-rich, dappled soil.

directions: Set the rhizomes horizontally a couple of inches deep in leafy soil and keep them cool and moist in spring. Let the foliage die back naturally as the plant goes dormant for summer. Soft lavender-blue flowers with a paler reverse open in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn under deciduous shade.

directions: Lay the rhizomes flat about two inches deep in moisture-retentive woodland soil. Keep the ground cool through the spring growing period and allow summer dormancy. Large soft blue flowers with a rosy reverse appear in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in cool, leaf-rich soil.

directions: Set the rhizomes horizontally two inches deep and keep evenly moist during spring growth. This slower-spreading double is prized for its neat button center. Pure white flowers with a tight central pompon of petals open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in humus-rich woodland soil.

directions: Lay the rhizomes flat a couple of inches deep and keep the soil cool and moist in spring. Let foliage yellow and die back for summer dormancy. Fully double white flowers open in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in leaf-rich shade.

directions: Set the rhizomes horizontally two inches deep in cool woodland soil. This curiosity replaces its petals with frilled green tufts rather than typical flowers. The unusual green rosettes appear in mid spring and last well.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in cool, moist woodland soil.

directions: Lay the rhizomes flat two inches deep and keep moist through the spring growth period. Prized for the blue tint at the heart of each bloom. Double white flowers with a blue-flushed center open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in leafy, dappled soil.

directions: Set the rhizomes horizontally a couple of inches deep and keep the soil cool and moist. This distinctive form has a green ruff of leafy bracts framing the flower. White petals streaked with green appear in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in humus-rich shade.

directions: Lay the rhizomes flat two inches deep in cool woodland soil and keep evenly moist in spring. Each flower carries a small green tuft at its center for a quirky look. White blooms topped with a green eye open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in cool, moist woodland soil.

directions: Lay the rhizomes flat two inches deep and keep moist during spring growth. This double form holds its color longer than the single. Fully double buttercup-yellow flowers open in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn in humus-rich, dappled woodland soil.

directions: Set the rhizomes horizontally two inches deep in cool, leafy soil and keep evenly moist. This natural hybrid of the white and yellow wood anemones spreads gently. Delicate pale sulphur-yellow flowers open in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Plant soaked tubers in autumn in well-drained soil.

directions: Soak the tubers overnight and set them two to three inches deep in gritty, free-draining soil. They spread into carpets beneath deciduous trees and shrubs. Large pure white daisy flowers with a pink-tinged reverse open in early spring.

planting.note: Plant soaked tubers in autumn in well-drained soil in sun or dappled shade.

directions: Soak tubers overnight before planting them two to three inches deep. This seed strain gives a range of tones from soft to deep blue. Daisy-like flowers open in early spring, then the plants rest through summer.

planting.note: Plant soaked tubers in autumn in free-draining soil.

directions: Soak the tubers overnight and plant two to three inches deep in well-drained ground. The striking two-tone flowers stand out at the front of a spring bed. Vivid magenta petals with a bold white eye open in early spring.

planting.note: Plant soaked tubers in autumn in well-drained soil.

directions: Soak the tubers overnight, then plant them two to three inches deep in gritty soil. They naturalize into soft drifts over the years. Clear pink daisy flowers with a pale center open in early spring.

planting.note: Plant soaked tubers in autumn in free-draining soil in light shade.

directions: Soak the tubers overnight and set them two to three inches deep in well-drained soil. Combine with white and blue forms for a colorful spring carpet. Deep rose-pink daisy flowers open in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in part shade.

directions: Set the crown flush with the soil and keep it evenly moist through summer. Leave the clump undisturbed to build a strong flowering stand. Soft pink single to semi-double flowers open from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring or sow outdoors once the soil warms after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the surface lightly moist until sprouts appear in two to three weeks. Give it lean, sharply drained soil and full sun, and shear the whole clump back hard after the first flush to spark rebloom. Golden-yellow daisies open from early summer and keep coming through late summer if spent stems are cut.

planting.note: Set out divisions or rooted cuttings in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before in gritty, free-draining soil and full sun. This selection carries soft lemon-yellow daisies rather than deep gold, so cut it back hard after flowering to keep the clump tidy and encourage a second show. Deadhead regularly and lift and split every second or third year to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant out divisions in spring after the last frost into warm, drained soil.

directions: Set plants in lean, well-drained soil in full sun and avoid rich feeding, which makes the stems flop. This old form bears bright deep-yellow daisies over ferny foliage from early to midsummer. Shear the spent flowers to the base to prompt fresh growth and a lighter second flush.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring once the ground has warmed.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, low-fertility soil in full sun for the sturdiest stems. This selection opens in a soft creamy pale yellow that fades almost to ivory, a gentler note than the golden types. Cut the plant back after the first bloom and take basal cuttings in late summer, since it is short-lived and does not come true from seed.

planting.note: Divide or plant out in spring after frosts have finished.

directions: Give this plant full sun and thin, gritty soil, and stake loosely in exposed spots. It produces pale creamy-yellow daisies over aromatic fern-like foliage across the summer. Deadhead often and cut back hard once the main flush fades to renew the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Grow in poor to average soil with excellent drainage and full sun. This form carries some of the deepest golden-yellow daisies in the group, held on upright stems above ferny leaves. Shear spent flowers to keep it blooming and divide every few years to prevent the center from dying out.

planting.note: Set out divisions or cuttings in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun and sharply drained soil, keeping fertility low so stems stay upright. The daisies open a warm golden-orange over grey-green ferny foliage through summer. Cut the plant back after the main flush and lift and divide regularly, as it tends to be short-lived.

planting.note: Sow indoors in early spring or direct-sow when the soil warms.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface and barely cover, keeping it lightly moist until it germinates in two to three weeks. Grow in full sun and lean, well-drained ground; this species stands out for its vivid deep orange daisies. It is often short-lived, so let a few heads set seed or take cuttings to keep it going.

planting.note: Plant out young plants in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Set plants in full sun and free-draining soil; this hybrid brings silvery, finely cut foliage and white daisies with soft yellow centers. Shear it back after the first flush to keep growth compact and prompt more bloom. Take cuttings in late summer to overwinter insurance plants, since it can be short-lived in cold, wet ground.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; propagate mainly from cuttings for named stock.

directions: Grow this spreading mat in full sun and gritty, sharply drained soil where it will not sit wet in winter. It forms silvery, aromatic filigree foliage topped by white daisies with gold eyes, mostly in late spring. Trim it back after flowering to keep it dense, and root cuttings in summer as backup in colder gardens.

planting.note: Sow in spring; this mat-former also comes readily from division.

directions: Site this low, silver-leaved species in full sun with fast-draining, gritty soil, ideal for a rock garden or gravel edge. Clusters of small bright-yellow daisies rise just above the ferny silver mat in early summer. Keep it lean and dry, and divide congested mats in spring to renew them.

planting.note: Sow in spring in a gritty mix; suited to alpine and rock plantings.

directions: Grow this compact alpine in full sun and very sharply drained, gritty soil that stays dry in winter. It makes low tufts of grey-green foliage topped with white daisies and yellow centers in early summer. Protect from wet with a gravel mulch and avoid rich feeding, which loosens the neat habit.

planting.note: Sow in spring; a low mat-former good at the front of a bed or in troughs.

directions: Plant this ground-hugging species in full sun and free-draining soil where it can form a low cushion. Large white daisies with golden eyes sit close over the toothed green foliage in early summer. Shear lightly after bloom to keep it tidy, and give it excellent drainage to survive damp winters.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring after frost; grow from cuttings for true stock.

directions: Give this vigorous selection full sun and sharply drained soil with room to spread. It bears large white daisies with yellow centers above ferny green foliage over a long summer season. Cut it back after the main flush to renew growth, and root late-summer cuttings since it can be tender and short-lived.

planting.note: Direct-sow in spring; it also self-seeds freely once established.

directions: Scatter seed on open, disturbed ground in spring and rake in lightly, as it favors poor, well-drained soil. This annual wildflower produces cheerful white daisies with yellow centers and, unlike stinking chamomile, has only faintly scented foliage. Leave a few heads to ripen if you want it to return, since it reseeds readily.

planting.note: Direct-sow in spring on open ground; expect vigorous self-seeding.

directions: Sow shallowly on disturbed, well-drained soil in spring, keeping in mind this is a weedy annual that spreads quickly. It carries small white daisies with yellow disks over finely divided foliage that gives off a sharp, unpleasant smell when bruised. Cut or pull plants before seed sets if you want to limit its spread.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors after a cold-moist chilling period, then set plants out around the last frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface of moist soil and leave it uncovered, since light aids germination. Give plants humus-rich, well-drained soil and steady moisture through their first season. Nodding blue to violet flowers open in late spring, and self-sown seedlings often carry the colony forward.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after cold stratification and transplant around last frost.

directions: Surface-sow chilled seed and keep it lightly moist and cool until sprouts appear. Plant in gritty, well-drained soil and shelter from afternoon heat in warm regions. The large sky-blue and white flowers with long backward spurs open in late spring.

planting.note: Start compact plants indoors and move to the rock garden after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it evenly moist and cool during germination. This dwarf Japanese species has thick blue-green fan-shaped leaves and suits rock gardens and edging. Short-spurred blue-and-white flowers nod above the tidy mound in late spring.

planting.note: Cold-stratify and start indoors; set into cool, gritty soil after frost.

directions: Sow seed on the surface after a period of moist cold and keep it lightly shaded. This mountain species prefers sharp drainage and cool summers. Large, deep-blue nodding flowers appear in early summer on relatively short stems.

planting.note: Sow in fall for natural winter chilling, or stratify before spring sowing.

directions: Surface-sow the seed in autumn or cold-treat it before a spring sowing. This West Coast native thrives in dappled light with consistent spring moisture. Scarlet-and-yellow spurred flowers hang from wiry stems and attract hummingbirds through late spring.

planting.note: Start indoors after chilling and transplant the small plants after frost.

directions: Press the seed onto moist soil and keep cool and lightly shaded until it sprouts. This petite species is grown for its unusual green-and-chocolate-brown blooms and their sweet scent. Flowers open in late spring on compact plants that suit the front of a border.

planting.note: Start indoors after cold treatment; this warm-climate species goes out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the chilled seed and keep it evenly moist through germination. Native to the desert Southwest, it tolerates heat given afternoon shade and good drainage. Pale-yellow flowers with dramatically long, trailing spurs bloom from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow after a moist-cold spell and keep the seedbed lightly shaded. This European species carries deep purple-black nodding flowers with prominent yellow stamens. Blooms arrive in late spring and self-sow freely in relaxed cottage plantings.

planting.note: Start alpine seed indoors after chilling and plant into sharply drained scree.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and give it cold, moist conditions before warming to germinate. This true alpine miniature needs gritty, fast-draining soil and cool air to thrive. Tiny blue-and-white nodding flowers top the low tuft in early summer.

planting.note: Chill seed first; this cold-loving species dislikes hot, humid summers.

directions: Surface-sow the chilled seed and keep it cool and evenly moist. This Siberian mountain species offers unusually large blue-and-white flowers on short stems. Give it excellent drainage and cool summers, and expect bloom in early summer.

planting.note: Start dwarf alpine seed indoors after chilling; plant into gritty rock-garden soil.

directions: Surface-sow after cold stratification and keep the seedlings cool. This diminutive Italian alpine bears large deep-blue flowers relative to its tiny size. Provide sharp drainage and shelter from summer heat, and it flowers in late spring.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out after frost; this Mexican species prefers milder winters.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it warm and moist to germinate. This tall species from Mexico carries nodding red-and-green flowers with long straight spurs. It flowers over a long summer season and is a favorite of hummingbirds.

planting.note: Cold-stratify, start indoors, and plant out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow chilled seed and keep it lightly moist until germination. This East Asian species bears purplish-brown and yellow nodding flowers in late spring. Grow it in humus-rich soil with steady moisture and part shade.

planting.note: Chill seed and start indoors; extremely cold-hardy once established.

directions: Surface-sow the seed after a moist-cold period and keep it cool. This hardy species from Siberia carries lilac-blue flowers with short, hooked spurs. It withstands severe winters and blooms in late spring.

planting.note: Start indoors after chilling and set out around the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and leave it uncovered, since light helps it break dormancy. This classic double, spurless selection carries pompon flowers of pink tipped with green. Blooms open in late spring, and seedlings come fairly true to the frilly form.

planting.note: Start indoors after cold treatment; transplant after frost.

directions: Press seed onto moist soil and keep uncovered and cool until it sprouts. This double, spurless Barlow selection produces deep maroon-black pompon flowers on tall stems. Flowers appear in late spring and are striking against silvery companions.

planting.note: Chill seed, start indoors, and plant out around last frost.

directions: Surface-sow and leave the seed exposed to light to encourage germination. This double, spurless Barlow form bears soft rose-pink pompon blooms fading paler at the edges. Expect flowers in late spring on airy, upright stems.

planting.note: Start indoors after chilling and set out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and evenly moist during germination. This double Barlow selection shows deep violet-blue petals neatly edged in white. The frilly, spurless flowers open in late spring above ferny foliage.

planting.note: Cold-stratify, start indoors, and plant out around last frost.

directions: Press seed onto damp soil, leave it uncovered, and keep it cool to germinate. Also sold as 'Magpie', this selection has dramatic dark purple flowers rimmed in white. The bold two-tone bells appear in late spring on tall, slender stems.

planting.note: Start indoors after chilling and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it lightly moist and cool until sprouting. This double selection produces richly colored dark wine-red pompon flowers. Blooms open in late spring and mix well with chartreuse or silver foliage.

planting.note: Cold-stratify and start indoors; plant out around last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and leave it uncovered to catch the light it needs. This double, spurless form opens apple-green and matures to creamy white pompons. The unusual blooms appear in late spring and last well as cut flowers.

planting.note: Start indoors after chilling and set out around last frost.

directions: Press the seed onto moist soil and keep it uncovered and cool to germinate. This double, spurless Barlow bears violet-blue pompon flowers on strong stems. The starry double blooms open in late spring and are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Start early indoors for possible first-year bloom; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed indoors in late winter and keep it cool and lightly moist. This popular hybrid strain offers large, long-spurred flowers in a wide mix of bicolors. Bloom comes in late spring, often the first year when started early enough.

planting.note: Start early indoors after chilling; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and evenly moist to germinate. This long-spurred selection carries crimson-red flowers with contrasting white centers. Bloom arrives in late spring on upright stems that suit cutting and borders.

planting.note: Start early indoors for possible first-year flowers; plant out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors in late winter and keep the seed cool and lightly moist. This Songbird series selection bears large, long-spurred sky-blue and white flowers. Blooms open in late spring on sturdy, upward-facing stems.

planting.note: Start early indoors; transplant after frost for late-spring bloom.

directions: Surface-sow seed and keep it cool and moist until it germinates. This Songbird selection carries large red-and-white flowers with long, elegant spurs. The upward-facing blooms open in late spring and draw hummingbirds.

planting.note: Start early indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and evenly moist while germinating. This Songbird selection produces clean, pure-white long-spurred flowers. The upward-facing blooms brighten shade borders in late spring.

common_name: Winky Double Red and White Columbine

planting.note: Start early indoors for compact first-year plants; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and lightly moist to germinate. This dwarf Winky-series selection holds its double red-and-white flowers facing upward and outward. Compact and tidy, it blooms in late spring and suits containers and small beds.

planting.note: Start early indoors and plant out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and evenly moist during germination. This compact Winky selection carries upward-facing blue-and-white flowers on short, sturdy stems. Its tidy habit makes it a good choice for pots and border fronts, blooming in late spring.

planting.note: Start early indoors; set out after frost for late-spring bloom.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and lightly moist to germinate. This Clementine-series selection bears fully double, upward-facing salmon-rose flowers without spurs. The clematis-like blooms open in late spring on compact plants.

planting.note: Start early indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and evenly moist while germinating. This Clementine selection produces double, spurless violet-blue flowers that face upward. The clematis-flowered blooms appear in late spring on neat, compact plants.

planting.note: Start dwarf plants indoors after chilling; set out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and lightly moist until sprouting. This very dwarf fan columbine forms a compact tuft ideal for troughs and rock gardens. Blue-and-white flowers nod just above the blue-green foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Start dwarf seed indoors after chilling and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow and keep the seed cool and evenly moist during germination. This dwarf white-flowered fan columbine has thick blue-green foliage and a tidy habit. Pure-white nodding flowers open in late spring, perfect for the rock garden.

planting.note: Cold-stratify, start indoors, and plant out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the chilled seed and keep it lightly moist and cool. This compact form of the native red columbine keeps the species' charm at half the height. Red-and-yellow lantern-shaped flowers dangle over the mound in mid-spring and lure hummingbirds.

planting.note: Cold-stratify and start indoors; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and give it a cold, moist start before warming to germinate. This soft-yellow selection of the native columbine stays compact and blooms freely. The pale lemon lantern flowers appear in mid-spring and self-sow gently.

planting.note: Start early indoors after chilling; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and lightly moist to germinate. This selection of golden columbine offers bright, long-spurred clear-yellow flowers on tall stems. Heat-tolerant and long-blooming, it flowers from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Start early indoors for possible first-year bloom; plant out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors in late winter and keep the seed cool and lightly moist. This Origami-series selection is compact and free-flowering with upward-facing red-and-white blooms. Well-branched plants flower over a long stretch beginning in late spring.

planting.note: Start early indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and evenly moist until germination. This Swan-series selection carries large, long-spurred blue-and-white flowers on well-branched plants. Vigorous and uniform, it blooms freely from late spring.

planting.note: Start indoors after chilling and set out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and lightly moist to germinate. This selection is grown as much for its gold-flecked variegated foliage as for its purple flowers. The bright leaves show best in part shade, and blooms open in late spring.

planting.note: Start early indoors for compact plants; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and evenly moist until it germinates. This compact strain holds clusters of upward-facing bicolor flowers in a mix of blue, pink, and white. Its dwarf habit suits containers and border edges, blooming in late spring.

planting.note: Start early indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and lightly moist during germination. This long-spurred selection carries deep blue and white star-form flowers held facing outward. Blooms open in late spring on upright stems that make good cut flowers.

planting.note: Start early indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and evenly moist to germinate. This selection produces clean, pure-white long-spurred flowers that brighten shade plantings. The crisp white blooms open in late spring and are lovely for cutting.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors in late winter or set out young plants around the last spring frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface of gritty, well-drained mix and barely cover, keeping it lightly moist until sprouts appear. Plant into lean, alkaline soil with sharp drainage and shear the mat back hard after flowering to keep it tidy. Fragrant white flowers blanket the foliage in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or plant plugs out near the last frost.

directions: Sow on the surface of a gritty mix and keep barely damp until germination, then grow on cool and bright. Site in full sun with excellent drainage and trim spent flowers to encourage a dense cushion. Expect a sheet of pure white bloom in spring atop grey-green foliage.

planting.note: Set out divisions or cuttings in spring or early autumn.

directions: Because the flowers are sterile doubles, propagate by lifting and dividing clumps or by rooting soft cuttings in early summer. Plant into sharply drained, sunny ground and shear after bloom to keep the mound compact. The showy double white flowers resemble tiny stocks and last longer than the single form in spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring for stable variegation.

directions: Propagate vegetatively from cuttings or division to hold the cream-edged leaf pattern, since seedlings revert to plain green. Grow in full sun with lean soil, removing any all-green shoots as they appear. Cream-margined foliage carries white spring flowers and stays attractive as ground cover the rest of the year.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or plant young stock at the last frost.

directions: Sprinkle seed on the surface of gritty compost and keep just moist and cool until it germinates. Plant into sun-baked, well-drained soil and clip the mat after flowering to renew the cushion. Rich rose-pink flowers cover the low mound throughout mid-spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or transplant plugs around the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and press it into contact with the mix, holding moisture until seedlings show. Give it a hot, gritty, sunny spot and shear spent bloom to prevent flopping. Soft pink flowers open in spring above tidy grey-green rosettes.

common_name: Little Treasure Deep Rose Rock Cress

planting.note: Start this compact strain indoors in late winter for spring planting.

directions: Sow on the surface of a fine, well-drained mix and keep it lightly moist and cool for even germination. This dwarf selection stays neat, so plant it at the front of a bed or in a trough with sharp drainage. Deep rose blooms smother the tight cushion in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors in late winter and set out around the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of gritty compost and keep just damp until sprouting, then grow cool and bright. This compact form suits edging, rock crevices, and containers with fast drainage. Crisp white flowers blanket the mounded foliage in spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors late winter; this compact pink strain flowers young.

directions: Surface-sow and keep barely moist, growing seedlings on in cool, bright conditions. This dense, low selection is ideal tucked between paving or along a wall top in full sun. Bright pink flowers cover the compact mat through spring.

planting.note: Start this uniform seed strain indoors in late winter.

directions: Sow on the mix surface and press in without covering, keeping cool and evenly moist until germination. Bred for uniformity, it makes tidy plugs for edging and rock gardens in full sun. Masses of white flowers appear reliably in the first spring after an early sowing.

planting.note: Direct-sow in spring or start indoors; it self-sows once established.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of gritty soil and firm it in, keeping lightly moist until seedlings emerge. This tough alpine tolerates poor, stony ground and cold; cut it back after flowering to limit self-seeding. Small white flowers open on loose stems in spring.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; less cold-hardy than wall rock cress.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it cool and just moist until sprouts appear, then grow on bright. This California native wants full sun and excellent drainage and dislikes winter wet. Fragrant rosy-purple flowers rise above fringed rosettes in early spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and set out after frost danger eases.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a well-drained mix and keep barely damp until germination, growing on cool. Give this selection a sunny, sharply drained site and avoid soggy soil over winter. Bright rose-pink flowers flush over compact rosettes in spring.

planting.note: Begin indoors in late winter; transplant once frosts have passed.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep cool and evenly moist until seedlings appear, then harden off gradually. Plant into full sun with fast drainage and protect from standing winter moisture. Deep reddish-pink flowers make a vivid low carpet in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; runners root as they spread.

directions: Set out divisions or rooted runners in well-drained soil, spacing them to knit into a low evergreen carpet. It tolerates light shade and spreads by creeping stems, so give it room to run over walls and slopes. Airy white flowers rise a few inches above the glossy mat in late spring.

planting.note: Propagate by division to keep the variegated foliage true.

directions: Divide established mats in spring or autumn to hold the cream-and-green leaf coloring, which seedlings will not reproduce. Plant in well-drained soil in sun to light shade and remove any plain-green runners. Glossy variegated leaves take on pink winter tints, and short spikes of white flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Usually planted from divisions into a rock garden or crevice.

directions: Set divisions into gritty, sharply drained soil where the tight rosettes can form a flat evergreen carpet. This Balkan alpine wants full sun and resents winter wet, so a raised bed or trough suits it well. Short stems of small white flowers appear over the mat in spring.

planting.note: Divide or take cuttings to keep the gold variegation stable.

directions: Propagate vegetatively from division or cuttings, as seed will not carry the yellow-gold leaf margins. Grow in full sun with excellent drainage; the bright foliage is the main feature year-round. Small white flowers rise on short stems in spring above the gold-edged mat.

planting.note: Sow in a cold frame; germination is slow and benefits from cool spells.

directions: Sow onto gritty alpine mix and keep cool, as this cushion-forming species germinates slowly and unevenly. Grow it in a scree bed, trough, or crevice with very sharp drainage and full sun. Tiny white flowers cluster just above the dense rosettes in late spring.

planting.note: Sow in a gritty alpine mix and expect slow, uneven germination.

directions: Surface-sow onto a lean, sharply drained alpine mix and keep cool, as this high-mountain species is slow to establish. It forms a silvery, moss-like cushion best grown in a trough or crevice with full sun and grit. A few small white flowers sit close over the tight cushion in early summer.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow on the surface and press in gently, as light aids germination, then keep evenly moist. This broader-leaved thrift forms a lush rosette topped by tall stems of rounded flower heads. Deadhead regularly and divide clumps every few years; blooms carry from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Set young plants into a scree or rock-garden pocket in spring.

directions: Sow into a very gritty mix and give the seed a cold spell to break dormancy before warmth. This tiny Spanish cushion demands perfect drainage and resents winter wet, so plant it in raised scree or a trough. Short-stemmed pink flowers cover the prickly bun in late spring.

planting.note: Start under cover and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Cover the seed lightly and keep it cool and moist until it germinates. This taller, broad-leaved thrift takes lean, well-drained ground and tolerates coastal exposure. Remove faded heads to prolong the display; pale to deep pink globes appear from early summer.

planting.note: Plant into a sunny rock garden or trough once soils warm.

directions: A short cold-moist period helps this mountain species sprout evenly from surface-sown seed. Give it gritty, fast-draining soil and full exposure, mimicking its high-altitude home. Compact tufts throw up short stems of pink flowers in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pots in spring after the ground thaws.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in sharp-draining ground and water sparingly once rooted. This selection is prized for its vivid magenta-pink flower balls held above tidy evergreen grass. Shear after the main flush to keep the mound neat and encourage rebloom from late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, freely draining spot.

directions: Plant the division so the grassy crown sits at the surface and avoid heavy, wet soil. Clean white pompons top the dark evergreen cushion, a crisp contrast in edging and rockeries. Trim off spent blooms to tidy the plant; flowering peaks in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the bronze foliage catches full sun.

directions: Set out in gritty soil and full light, which deepens the reddish-purple leaf color. The dark grassy mound carries pink flower heads that pop against the burgundy foliage. Shear lightly after bloom to refresh the cushion; expect flowers in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Place the crown at soil level in lean, quick-draining ground and keep water modest. This dependable German selection bears rich rose-red flower globes over compact evergreen tufts. Deadhead through the season to extend bloom, which starts in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; propagate only by division to keep the variegation.

directions: Set the division into gritty soil in full sun and never let it stand wet. The draw here is the creamy-edged evergreen foliage that shows year-round, topped by pink flowers. Remove any all-green reverting shoots and shear spent stems; blooms come in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sharply drained position.

directions: Keep the crown at grade in fast-draining soil and water only when dry. This compact form bears fluffy white flower heads over a low green cushion. Snip off faded blooms to tidy the tuft; the main show is in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun for the deepest color.

directions: Settle the crown at soil level in lean, gritty ground and go easy on watering. Named for its deep blood-red flower globes carried on wiry stems above grassy mounds. Deadhead to keep it blooming and shear late-season straggle; first flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a bright, well-drained spot.

directions: Set the division in sharp-draining soil and full sun, keeping moisture low. This old favorite is compact and free-flowering, with rich rose-pink globes over dense green tufts. Shear spent flowers for a tidy mound and possible rebloom; peak color is late spring.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for first-year bloom.

directions: Surface-sow and press in lightly, keeping cool and moist until seedlings emerge. This seed strain is quick and uniform, flowering the first season if started early. Deadhead to prolong the deep rose display over compact grassy mounds through late spring and summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for bloom the first year.

directions: Press the fine seed onto the surface and keep it lightly moist at room temperature. Vigorous and even, this white strain often blooms its first summer from an early sowing. Snip spent heads to keep the clean white globes coming over neat evergreen tufts.

planting.note: Start under lights in late winter and transplant after frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and hold at cool room temperature until germination. This tidy seed series is well matched for size and bloom, showing rose-pink globes over grassy clumps. Deadhead to lengthen the season and grow lean for the sturdiest habit; blooms in late spring.

planting.note: Raise indoors in late winter for planting out in spring.

directions: Sow on the surface, press gently, and keep evenly moist until seedlings show. The white member of this uniform series carries clean globes on short, even stems. Remove faded flowers to keep it blooming and plant in sharp-draining soil; peak is late spring.

planting.note: Start indoors early; this series flowers the first year.

directions: Surface-sow and give bright warmth, as light and mild heat speed this quick strain. Bred to bloom the first season, it forms neat rosettes topped by large red flower heads. Deadhead often to keep the compact plant reflowering from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for first-year flowers.

directions: Press seed onto the surface under bright light and keep warm until sprouting. This dwarf, first-year-flowering strain lifts big white globes above tidy green rosettes. Trim spent blooms to prompt more; flowering runs from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early for bloom the first summer.

directions: Sow on the surface in warmth and light and keep the medium just moist. Compact and early, this selection shows soft lilac-pink flower heads over green rosettes. Regular deadheading extends the display from late spring well into summer.

planting.note: Raise indoors and set out after the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow under bright warmth and keep lightly moist until germination. This taller series lifts big flower globes on long, wiry stems, good for cutting. Deadhead to prolong the vivid red bloom that carries from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for tall summer stems.

directions: Press the seed onto the surface and keep warm and bright to trigger germination. Long stems carry large white pompons above the rosette, holding well in a vase. Cut or deadhead spent stems to keep flowers coming through late spring and summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and plant out after frost.

directions: Sow on the surface in warmth with good light and keep the mix evenly damp. Tall stems bear large globes in a blend of lilac and pink tones, fine for cutting. Deadhead regularly to sustain the long bloom from late spring into midsummer.

planting.note: Plant potted stock in spring; propagate by division only.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in a bright, well-drained bed. This vegetative selection is a strong rebloomer, sending up rounded flower heads over a full rosette. Shear off spent stems to trigger fresh flushes from late spring into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and sharp drainage.

directions: Place the crown at grade in freely draining soil and water moderately once settled. A tidy vegetative form with two-tone pink flower globes carried above lush foliage. Remove faded heads to encourage repeat bloom through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring; keep to division to hold the selection true.

directions: Set out in spring with the crown at soil level in bright, well-drained ground. This compact clone reblooms freely, lifting soft pink globes above a dense green rosette. Deadhead spent stems to keep the flushes coming from late spring into autumn.

planting.note: Plant into scree or a trough in spring.

directions: Nestle the small division into very gritty, fast-draining soil and avoid winter wet. This tight cushion is smothered in deep rose-pink flowers on very short stems. Ideal for troughs and rock crevices; the main flush comes in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a raised, sharply drained pocket.

directions: Tuck the division into scree or a gritty trough and keep it dry over winter. This miniature bun carries near-stemless white flowers pressed against the prickly foliage. Perfect for alpine collections; expect the bloom peak in late spring.

planting.note: Set out divisions or nursery plants in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant in gritty, sharply drained soil with the crown at ground level, spacing mounds about a foot apart. Shear the cushion back hard if it flops or splits open in midsummer heat, and avoid rich soil or overwatering. Grown for its silky silver foliage rather than the muted yellow flowers, so trim blooms to keep the tidy dome.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; contain the roots where it may spread.

directions: Surface-sow seed or set divisions in poor, dry soil where spreading rhizomes have room to roam or a buried barrier to check them. Cut stems to the ground in late winter and shear midseason if the clump opens up. Native to prairies, it colors best in silver where soil is lean and sun is strong.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring; give it room or a root barrier.

directions: Plant divisions in lean, well-drained soil where its running roots can spread into a silver drift. Cut back hard in spring and shear again if stems sprawl in summer. The narrow willowy foliage dries well, making it a classic filler for wreaths and everlasting bouquets.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring, allowing space for spread.

directions: Plant this broader-leaved selection in dry, well-drained ground and full sun for the whitest foliage. Shear back by half in midsummer to keep it dense and upright. Like other running wormwoods, corral the roots if you want it to stay in bounds.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and sharp drainage.

directions: Set out plants in lean soil with the crown level and space them for spread, since the rhizomes wander. Its broad silver leaves with clean scalloped edges are the draw, so cut back the flowering stems and shear midseason to hold the shape. Divide every few years to renew vigor.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sandy, fast-draining soil.

directions: A tough seaside plant, set it in sandy or gravelly soil where salt spray and drought would defeat lesser perennials. Plant the crown at grade and let the felted white stems creep into a low mat. Shear off flower stalks to keep the foliage tidy and compact.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants in spring in sharp drainage.

directions: Plant this low, oak-leaf-shaped selection as a silver groundcover or edger in dry, well-drained soil. Trim back trailing stems to keep it dense, and remove flower stalks so energy stays in the woolly foliage. It tolerates heat, salt, and drought once established.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: This prostrate silver form spills nicely over walls and along path edges in lean, dry soil. Set rooted plants at grade and pinch back stems to encourage branching. Shear away flowering shoots to preserve the low, ferny carpet of white foliage.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out around the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and press it in, since light aids germination, then transplant to poor, well-drained soil. Cut the plant back hard in spring to keep it bushy and prevent a woody base. The aromatic silver-gray foliage is the classic bitter herb of absinthe and holds well as an ornamental.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in dry, sunny ground.

directions: A refined selection of wormwood with especially finely cut silver leaves, best grown from cuttings in lean soil. Prune back by half in early spring to renew the foliage and keep it from splaying. Give it full sun and sharp drainage for the brightest silver color.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring; site carefully as it spreads aggressively.

directions: Surface-sow seed on average soil, or plant divisions, keeping in mind that spreading roots and self-seeding make it weedy in many regions. Cut it back before it sets seed to limit its spread. Grown historically as a bitter herb, its dark green, silver-backed leaves have an upright, architectural presence.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; a container or barrier keeps it contained.

directions: This variegated mugwort splashes chartreuse and green across its leaves and is grown for foliage color, not bloom. Because it runs vigorously, plant it in a sunken pot or root barrier and shear repeatedly to refresh the coloring. Cut to the ground in late winter for a flush of bright new growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moisture-retentive soil, unlike its dry-loving relatives.

directions: Unusual among wormwoods, this species is grown for its airy plumes of creamy white flowers on tall dark stems. Plant in rich, moist soil in sun to light shade and stake the tall stalks in exposed sites. The fragrant sprays open in late summer and make a fine cut flower.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-holding soil.

directions: This form pairs the creamy flower plumes of white mugwort with striking dark purple stems and bronze-flushed young foliage. Give it moist, rich soil and full sun to part shade, and stake if it grows leggy. The dusky stems and pale blooms provide strong late-summer contrast in a border.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in very well-drained, gritty soil.

directions: A compact prairie native, sow the fine seed on the surface of lean, gravelly soil and barely cover it. Extremely drought and cold tolerant, it forms a low mound of thread-fine silver foliage. Shear off the small yellow flower spikes if you prefer to keep the neat silvery cushion.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; give room for its running roots.

directions: This feathery, finely textured wormwood spreads by rhizomes into a soft silver-green haze. Plant in dry, well-drained soil and contain the roots where a tidy clump is wanted. Shear it in summer to renew the delicate foliage and remove the small nodding flowers.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Grown for its curling, thread-like silver foliage that catches light like frost, this selection thrives in poor, dry soil. Plant rooted cuttings in full sun and shear if the mound loosens. The intricate filigree texture makes it a standout edging or container plant.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants in spring in sharply drained soil.

directions: Named for its curling, coral-like silver foliage that swirls like sea foam, this low grower needs lean, fast-draining soil. Plant in full sun and avoid rich or wet ground, which causes it to melt out. Trim lightly to keep the intricate texture dense and mounded.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring once frost has passed.

directions: This shrubby Mediterranean wormwood grows into a large mound of bright silver, ferny foliage in mild climates. Grow it from cuttings in lean, well-drained soil and full sun, and shelter it from hard frost or overwinter cuttings indoors. Prune in spring to keep it dense, as it can grow rangy.

planting.note: Plant in fall so roots establish before the dry season in its native range.

directions: A signature shrub of California coastal scrub, it forms a soft mound of aromatic, thread-fine gray-green foliage. Plant in fast-draining soil and full sun, and once established water little to nothing through summer. It may drop leaves during extreme drought and releaf with fall rains.

planting.note: Sow seed in fall or plant in spring in very dry, alkaline soil.

directions: The iconic silver-gray shrub of the western sagebrush steppe, it needs sharp drainage, full sun, and very little water. Surface-sow the tiny seed on bare mineral soil, as it needs light and cool temperatures to sprout. Its three-toothed leaves release a distinctive sage scent, and airy flower spikes appear in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil as a groundcover.

directions: This mat-forming wormwood spreads into a low silver carpet ideal for slopes, edges, and between pavers. Plant in lean, sharply drained soil and full sun, spacing plants to knit together. Extremely drought tolerant once rooted, it needs only occasional trimming to stay neat.

planting.note: Set plants out in spring once the soil warms and stays reliably moist.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and press it into the mix without covering, as it needs light and a period of cold to break dormancy. Give the plant rich, consistently moist soil and afternoon shade in hot regions, and site it where its size will not crowd smaller neighbors. Tall creamy plumes rise above bold foliage in early summer; leave the seed heads for winter interest or cut them back after fading.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a bed where its low mound stays visible.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface of a moist mix and expose it to light and a cold spell to trigger germination. Grow this compact species in cool, humus-rich soil with steady moisture and shelter from midday sun. Short creamy plumes appear over ferny foliage in early summer, and the leaves turn orange-red as autumn arrives.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a moist, partly shaded spot with room to fill in.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface and keep it cool and lit, as burial and warmth suppress sprouting. Provide deep, fertile soil that never dries out and a site with dappled or morning sun. Feathery cream plumes open in early to midsummer above divided, somewhat glossy leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this variety stays more compact than the typical species.

directions: Scatter seed on a moist surface, leave it uncovered, and chill it before moving to warmth to prompt even germination. This smaller-statured goatsbeard wants cool, moist, organic soil and light shade in warm climates. Airy white plumes rise in early summer on a plant that reads as a tidier version of common goatsbeard.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall to keep the finely cut foliage true.

directions: Propagate this selection by dividing established clumps, since seedlings will not repeat the threadlike leaves. Set the crown at soil level in rich, moist ground and shade it from harsh afternoon sun. Delicate cream plumes float above the lacy, deeply dissected foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where the plant can reach full stature.

directions: Increase this cultivar by division to keep its form consistent, planting the divisions in deep, moisture-retentive soil. Give it partial shade in warm regions and steady water through summer to prevent leaf scorch. Bold ivory plumes stand above the dark green foliage from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this compact hybrid tolerates warmth better than the species.

directions: Plant this sterile hybrid from divisions or nursery pots, setting the crown flush with the soil in rich, moist ground. Its smaller size and better heat tolerance suit mixed borders where full goatsbeard would be too large. Airy white plumes appear over lacy, ferny foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where the reddish stems can be appreciated.

directions: Divide established plants to propagate this hybrid, planting into cool, moisture-retentive soil in light shade. Its wiry bronze-red stems carry pink-flushed buds that open to airy cream plumes in early summer. Leave the framework standing into autumn, when the foliage colors and the branching structure adds interest.

planting.note: Plant in spring near the front of a shaded border where its low habit shows.

directions: Propagate this compact selection by division, setting the crowns into humus-rich, evenly moist soil. Its dark, finely cut leaves form a neat mound suited to woodland edges and shaded plantings. Small cream plumes rise just above the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a moist, partly shaded bed.

directions: Divide clumps in spring or fall to keep this hybrid true to type, planting into deep, fertile, moist soil. Shade from hot afternoon sun and consistent water keep the ferny foliage fresh through the season. Feathery cream plumes open above the finely textured leaves in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this hybrid bridges the large and dwarf types.

directions: Increase this cross by division, planting into cool, organic, reliably moist soil in dappled shade. Its intermediate size and lacy foliage make it a versatile choice between full-size and dwarf goatsbeards. Airy white plumes rise over the ferny leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring toward the front of a moist shade bed.

directions: Propagate this compact selection by division rather than seed to preserve its habit. Grow it in humus-rich, consistently moist soil with protection from strong afternoon sun. Fluffy cream plumes appear above the fine, dark green foliage around midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring at a bed edge where the tidy mound stays in view.

directions: Surface-sow this seed-raised dwarf strain and keep it lit and cool to encourage even sprouting. Grow the compact clumps in moist, humus-rich soil with light shade in warm regions. Short cream plumes stand over ferny foliage in early summer, and the leaves take on warm tones in fall.

planting.note: Chill seed a month before an early indoor start, or sow directly in autumn.

directions: Plant into fast-draining, unimproved soil and set the crown at grade to prevent rot. Keep moisture low once established and resist moving the plant, as the taproot dislikes disturbance. This selection carries clear golden-yellow umbels from early summer, a bright break from the usual orange.

planting.note: Stratify seed cold and moist, then sow; this strain comes in mixed flower colors.

directions: Sow shallowly in gritty, well-drained ground and leave the seedlings in place to develop their taproot. Provide full sun and minimal water, avoiding rich soil that encourages floppy growth. This seed mix blooms in a blend of red, orange, and yellow through midsummer.

planting.note: Chill seed a month then start indoors, or divide established clumps in spring.

directions: Set plants in moisture-retentive soil in full sun and never let them dry out through summer. Cut spent stems in late winter to make room for fresh growth. This white-flowered selection blooms from midsummer and draws monarchs and swallowtails to its cool, crisp umbels.

planting.note: Cold-stratify then start indoors; happiest in consistently moist ground.

directions: Grow in damp, fertile soil and full sun, watering freely during dry spells. Leave the seed pods over winter for structure and self-sowing, or remove them to curb spread. Rich rose-pink, vanilla-scented flowers appear across mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start warm indoors without stratification and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Sow at warm room temperature and pot on before moving outdoors into full sun. Deadhead lightly to prolong flowering and cut back in autumn where it overwinters. This selection carries pure golden-yellow blooms all season and feeds monarch caterpillars readily.

planting.note: Stratify seed briefly or fall-sow; a drought-tolerant western native.

directions: Sow shallowly in lean, well-drained soil and water only to establish, since it handles heat and drought well. Its fine, whorled foliage suits dry gardens and native plantings. Pale pink to white flower clusters appear from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Cold-moist stratify seed a month; tolerates part shade at woodland edges.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in average, well-drained soil that stays lightly moist. Unlike sun-baked kin it accepts light shade and edges of woods. Deep rosy-purple flower clusters, richer than common milkweed, open in early summer.

planting.note: Stratify seed cold and moist; a well-behaved, non-aggressive prairie milkweed.

directions: Sow shallowly in rich to average soil and full sun in a moist prairie or border setting. It resembles common milkweed but stays tidier and clumps rather than running aggressively. Pink flower clusters and smooth blue-green leaves appear from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Stratify seed cold and moist; a woodland-edge species tolerant of shade.

directions: Sow in well-drained, humus-rich soil in part shade beneath open woods. It grows slowly and resents disturbance once the roots settle. Tight rounded clusters of white flowers, each ringed with purple at the center, open in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Light stratification helps; a heat-loving southern prairie milkweed with a deep taproot.

directions: Sow in lean, well-drained soil in full sun and leave in place, as the taproot resents moving. It tolerates heat, drought, and poor ground once established. Broad greenish flower clusters marked with purple centers appear from spring into summer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; a woolly, drought-adapted western species.

directions: Sow in sharply drained soil in full sun and keep it dry, as it goes dormant in summer heat. The whole plant is coated in soft white wool, an adaptation to sun and drought. Nodding clusters of deep pink to purple flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Stratify seed cold and moist; a heat- and drought-tolerant California native.

directions: Sow in dry, well-drained soil in full sun and water only until roots take hold. Its felted gray-green leaves shrug off intense heat and dry summers. Creamy white flower clusters bloom from late spring into summer, followed by woolly seed pods.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; a western foothill species for lean, rocky sites.

directions: Sow in rocky, fast-draining soil in full sun and keep it on the dry side. The blue-green, heart-shaped clasping leaves are handsome even out of bloom. Deep purple-red flower clusters appear in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; a prairie and glade species tolerant of poor soil.

directions: Sow in dry, well-drained soil in full sun on a prairie or rocky glade. It stands upright with narrow leaves and needs no supplemental water once rooted. Dense clusters of small greenish-white flowers open along the stem in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; a small woodland milkweed for shade.

directions: Sow in humus-rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade beneath deciduous trees. This dainty species is named for the whorl of four leaves at midstem. Loose clusters of pale pink flowers appear in late spring, then the plant fades quietly by late summer.

planting.note: Light chilling helps; a drought-tough Southwest species that spreads by rhizome.

directions: Sow in dry, well-drained soil in full sun and give room, since it colonizes by roots. Its fine whorled leaves resemble horsetail and handle heat and drought easily. Small white flower clusters bloom over a long summer season.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; a dry-prairie species for lean, well-drained ground.

directions: Sow in sandy or gravelly soil in full sun and keep conditions dry and lean. It handles drought and poor ground where showier milkweeds struggle. Rounded clusters of unusual all-green flowers hang from the stem in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Little chilling needed; a desert shrublet for hot, dry gardens.

directions: Sow in fast-draining, gritty soil in full sun and water sparingly, as it is built for desert heat. The needle-like foliage gives it a soft, evergreen shrub look year-round. Small white flower clusters appear off and on through the warm season.

planting.note: No chilling needed; a leafless desert species for xeric gardens.

directions: Sow in sharply drained, sandy soil in blazing full sun and give minimal water once established. Its upright, nearly leafless green stems form a striking clump for desert landscapes. Pale creamy-yellow flowers appear along the stems through much of the year in warm climates.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; a small northern-prairie milkweed.

directions: Sow in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade on a dry prairie or sandy site. This compact species spreads slowly by rhizome into small patches. Modest clusters of creamy-white flowers, sometimes pink-tinged, bloom in early summer.

planting.note: Light chilling helps; a tall, narrow-leaved Great Plains species.

directions: Sow in dry, well-drained soil in full sun on open prairie or a xeric border. Its thin, sparse leaves and wiry stems shrug off heat and drought. Nodding clusters of small greenish flowers appear in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; a slow, conservative tallgrass-prairie species that is rare in the wild.

directions: Sow in lean, well-drained prairie soil in full sun and expect slow, patient growth over several years to first bloom. It is a small, conservative species best suited to restoration and native plantings. A single nodding cluster of greenish-cream flowers appears in late spring.

planting.note: Plant potted stock or divisions in spring for bloom the same autumn.

directions: Set plants at the same depth they grew in the pot and water in well until established. This compact selection forms a tidy dome and rarely needs staking or pinching. Deep violet flowers smother the foliage in September and October.

planting.note: Divide or plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant in rich soil and keep evenly moist during summer to prevent lower leaf drop. Pinch once in June to reduce height and improve branching. Vivid salmon-pink daisies open in late summer and glow against the season's fading garden.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Space these dwarf plants closely for a solid edging and keep the soil evenly moist. Divide every couple of years to refresh the clump and limit mildew. Clear lavender-blue daisies with yellow eyes cover low mounds in September.

planting.note: Plant in spring in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants where they have room to form a broad, arching mound and avoid rich or wet soils. A single early-summer shearing keeps growth compact. Fragrant foliage gives way to a haze of lavender-blue daisies in October.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and sharp drainage.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil and let it dry between waterings once rooted. This selection stays more compact than the species and needs no staking. Sky-blue daisies blanket the mound from early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average, well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants in full sun and give tall stems space to stand without crowding. The waxy blue-green leaves shrug off powdery mildew that troubles other asters. Abundant violet-blue flowers appear from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring for dark foliage and fall bloom.

directions: Site in full sun to deepen the purple-black cast of the small leaves. Give it room, as it can self-sow and spread over time. Clouds of tiny white daisies with red eyes cover the dark stems in autumn.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, or plant divisions in spring.

directions: This tough prairie native thrives in dry, lean soils and full sun. Fine, needle-like leaves clothe wiry, spreading stems. Masses of small white daisies smother the plant in a snowy display through fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a groundcover or over walls.

directions: Use this prostrate selection as a creeping mat or let it spill over a wall or slope. It demands sharp drainage and tolerates drought once established. In fall the ground-hugging stems disappear beneath tiny white daisies.

planting.note: Stratify seed, or divide in spring; excellent shade groundcover.

directions: Grown as much for its large, heart-shaped basal leaves as its bloom, it forms a spreading groundcover in shade. Give it humus-rich, evenly moist soil beneath deciduous trees. Pale violet to white daisies rise on tall stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or start seed in spring; give it room.

directions: This towering aster forms bold clumps of large paddle-shaped leaves and needs space at the back of a border. It stands upright without staking despite its height. Flat clusters of lavender-blue daisies open very late, often through October and November.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a more compact form of the species.

directions: This shorter selection keeps the bold foliage of the species at a more manageable height. Give it full sun and average soil where it can spread into a stately clump. Lavender-blue daisy clusters crown the plant in mid to late autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained, ideally alkaline soil.

directions: This mildew-resistant European species prefers well-drained, limy soil and dislikes winter wet. It forms neat, bushy clumps that seldom need staking. Fragrant violet daisies with yellow centers bloom from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharply drained soil.

directions: Provide excellent drainage and avoid soggy winter conditions that can rot the crown. This old, reliable selection needs little pinching to stay compact. Large violet-blue daisies with golden eyes open freely in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; sterile hybrid grown from divisions.

directions: Prized for one of the longest bloom seasons of any aster, it flowers from midsummer until frost. Give it full sun, good drainage, and light staking if stems flop. Clear lavender-blue daisies with yellow centers appear over many weeks.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: This long-flowering hybrid blooms from summer to autumn and benefits from deadheading to prolong the display. Provide sun and sharp drainage, and stake in exposed sites. Soft lavender-blue daisies keep coming over an exceptionally long season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: This compact, uniform selection stays tidy and blooms heavily. Provide full sun and good drainage, especially through winter. Rich blue-purple flowers cover the mounds from early summer, and deadheading brings repeat bloom.
